content:
Course title: Introduction to Programming
Course code: CSE1100
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 1
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Software Engineering

Course description:
Introductory programming course focused on programming in Java. Covers primitive datatypes, operators, expressions, declarations, assignments, control structures such as conditional selection and repetition, data composition with arrays, Strings, ArrayLists, and Sets, class specification and implementation, equality and hashcodes, inheritance, type casting, polymorphism, static and dynamic binding, use of libraries (APIs), code quality, unit testing with JUnit, debugging strategies, exception handling, generics, basic input/output operations, design and implementation of small programs, and functional programming in Java using streams, map, and filter.

Main topics:
- Programming Fundamentals
- Object-Oriented Programming
- Data Structures
- Inheritance
- Functional Programming
- Exception Handling
- Unit Testing
- Input/Output Operations

Keywords:
Java, primitive datatypes, operators, control structures, arrays, ArrayList, Set, class specification, hashCode, polymorphism, static binding, dynamic binding, API, JUnit, streams

Course title: Reasoning and Logic
Course code: CSE1300
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 1
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Mathematics

Course description:
This course covers the principles of logical reasoning and argument validity. It introduces basic proof techniques such as proof by contradiction, mathematical induction, division into cases, and the use of invariants. Students learn to express statements precisely using artificial languages, specifically propositional calculus and predicate logic, including their syntax and semantics. The course covers translation between natural language and formal logic, and methods to establish logical validity in both propositional and predicate logic. Elementary set theory is included, with attention to set and graph operations, functions, relations, equivalence classes, and recursive definitions such as trees. The course also addresses constructing direct and indirect proofs, logical equivalence, counterexamples, and solving simple SAT instances.

Main topics:
- Propositional Logic
- Predicate Logic
- Proof Techniques
- Set Theory
- Graph Theory
- Functions and Relations
- Equivalence Classes
- Recursive Definitions

Keywords:
logical validity, proof by contradiction, mathematical induction, division into cases, invariants, propositional calculus, predicate logic, syntax, semantics, logical connectors, quantifiers, set operations, graph operations, recursive data structures, SAT

Course title: Computer Organisation
Course code: CSE1400
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 1
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Hardware & Architecture

Course description:
Covers the architecture, structure, operation, and interconnection of computer components in computer systems, including modern architectures. Topics include data representation, assembler programming, virtual machines, structure of translators, compiling and loading, and basic operating system concepts such as I/O, interrupt handling, and processes. Explores number representation systems, digital circuits, Boolean algebra, arithmetic operations, and the main components of digital computers: processing unit, I/O system, memory system, and interconnection system. Examines mechanisms for building faster processors, including pipelining, memory organization, caching, and parallelism, as well as trade-offs in performance, cost, scalability, availability, and energy consumption.

Main topics:
- Computer Architecture
- Digital Circuits
- Assembler Programming
- Operating Systems
- Boolean Algebra
- Data Representation
- Memory Organization
- Parallel Processing

Keywords:
number representation, digital computer, virtual machine, assembler, translators, compiling, loading, I/O system, interrupt handling, process, Boolean algebra, pipelining, caching, parallelism, memory system

Course title: Calculus
Course code: CSE1200
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 1
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Mathematics

Course description:
This course covers fundamental mathematical concepts essential for Computer Science and Engineering, including functions and their properties, limits and their calculation, differentiation and its rules such as the Chain Rule and implicit differentiation, and integration using Riemann sums, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, substitution, and integration by parts. It addresses sequences and series, including convergence tests and power series, as well as complex numbers and their algebraic properties, Euler's Identity, and De Moivre's Rule. The course also explores multivariate functions, including graphical representation, tangent planes, gradients, and local extrema, and the integration of functions of several variables over simple regions.

Main topics:
- Functions
- Limits
- Differentiation
- Integration
- Sequences and Series
- Complex Numbers
- Multivariate Functions
- Multivariate Integration

Keywords:
domain, range, inverse function, trigonometric functions, L'Hospital's Rule, Chain Rule, implicit differentiation, linearisation, differentials, Riemann sums, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, substitution rule, integration by parts, comparison test, alternating series test, ratio test, radius of convergence, Euler's Identity, De Moivre's Rule, tangent plane, directional derivative, gradient, local extrema

Course title: Collaborative Software Engineering Project
Course code: CSE1105
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 1
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Software Engineering

Course description:
This course covers the collaborative development of a software system in a team setting. Topics include interpreting client requirements, breaking them into actionable tasks, using collaboration tools such as Git and GitLab for version control and project management, and applying frameworks and libraries for project implementation. Students build a client/server application using Spring Boot for the server and JavaFX for the client, exchange data via REST with automated object mapping, and manage application lifecycle through dependency injection. The course emphasizes sustainable project work, code integration, testing, and peer code review.

Main topics:
- Software Engineering
- Teamwork
- Project Management
- Version Control
- Client-Server Architecture
- RESTful APIs
- Dependency Injection

Keywords:
Git, GitLab, Spring Boot, JavaFX, REST, Object Mapping, Merge Requests, Code Review, Project Backlog, Task Distribution, Testing, Source Code Integration, Dependency Injection, Client Requirements, Collaboration Tools

Course title: Web and Database Technology
Course code: CSE1500
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 1
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Software Engineering

Course description:
Introduction to web and database technologies, covering web architecture, HTTP, web development and application design. Focus on front-end development using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and back-end development with Node.js. Database topics include relational model, database design theory, SQL (data definition, constraints, updates, queries, views, triggers), relational databases, object persistence, and non-relational database models such as key-value stores, document stores, and graph databases. Emphasis on designing conceptual and logical database schemas, database normalization, and deploying database applications using open-source systems.

Main topics:
- Web Development
- Front-End Development
- Back-End Development
- Relational Databases
- Database Design
- SQL
- Non-Relational Databases
- Database Normalization

Keywords:
HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Node.js, HTTP, SQL, Database Schema, Functional Dependencies, Database Normalization, Relational Model, Key-Value Store, Document Store, Graph Database, Triggers, Open-Source Database

Course title: Linear Algebra
Course code: CSE1205
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 2
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Mathematics

Course description:
Solving systems of linear equations using Gaussian elimination, exploring vectors in R^n, and understanding linear dependence and independence. Covers matrix algebra including sum, product, inverse, and LU decomposition. Examines linear transformations, subspaces in R^n (basis, dimension), column space, null space, determinants, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and diagonalization. Includes inner products, orthogonal sets, orthogonal projections, least squares method, linear models, symmetric matrices, orthogonal diagonalization, and the spectral theorem.

Main topics:
- Linear Algebra
- Matrix Algebra
- Vector Spaces
- Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
- Linear Transformations
- Orthogonality
- LU Decomposition
- Diagonalization
- Least Squares

Keywords:
Gaussian elimination, vectors, R^n, linear dependence, matrix inverse, LU decomposition, subspace, column space, null space, determinant, eigenvalue, eigenvector, diagonalization, inner product, Gram-Schmidt process

Course title: Algorithms and Data Structures
Course code: CSE1305
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 2
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Software Engineering

Course description:
The course covers core data structures, starting with arrays and linked lists, and advancing to stacks, queues, priority queues, heaps, and maps. It addresses both iterative and recursive operations, focusing on analyzing time and space complexity. Sorting algorithms are studied in depth, including selection, insertion, heap, merge, quick, bucket, and radix sorts, with emphasis on implementation and complexity analysis. Searching is explored through balanced search structures such as generic search trees, AVL trees, and (2,4)-trees. The course concludes with graph theory, including directed and undirected graphs, weighted graphs, adjacency representations, and algorithms for traversals (breadth-first and depth-first), connectivity, cycle detection, topological sorting, single-source shortest paths, and minimum-spanning trees. Implementation is performed in Java.

Main topics:
- Data Structures
- Algorithm Analysis
- Sorting Algorithms
- Searching Algorithms
- Graph Theory
- Complexity Analysis
- Recursive Algorithms
- Java Programming

Keywords:
arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, priority queues, heaps, maps, selection sort, insertion sort, heap sort, merge sort, quick sort, bucket sort, radix sort, search trees, AVL trees, (2,4)-trees, graph traversal, breadth-first search, depth-first search, topological sorting, single-source shortest path, minimum-spanning tree, Java

Course title: Information and Data Management
Course code: CSE1505
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 2
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Data Science

Course description:
This course covers modeling, managing, and querying information in Database Management Systems (DBMS). It explores data modeling techniques, including normalization and the design of relational schemas using Entity Relationship diagrams. The course examines file organization and indexing, focusing on data structures and algorithms for efficient data storage and retrieval. Query processing and optimization are addressed, including relational algebra, SQL execution, and physical execution plans. Transaction management and concurrency control techniques are discussed to ensure data consistency and parallel operations. The course also introduces alternative database systems such as NewSQL, NoSQL, and systems optimized for OLAP or in-memory storage, covering document, key-value, wide-column, and graph data models. Students analyze trade-offs between different data models and system architectures, and study recovery algorithms like UNDO/REDO logging.

Main topics:
- Database Management Systems
- Data Modeling
- Relational Databases
- Query Processing
- Transaction Management
- Indexing
- NoSQL Databases
- Database Recovery

Keywords:
Entity Relationship diagrams, relational schema, normalization, SQL, relational algebra, file organization, index structures, query optimization, physical execution plan, transaction protocols, concurrency control, UNDO/REDO logging, NewSQL, OLAP, in-memory storage

Course title: Probability Theory and Statistics
Course code: CSE1210
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 2
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Mathematics

Course description:
Graphical and numerical summaries of data, introduction to probability, elementary combinatorics for probability calculation, conditional probability, Bayes' rule, stochastic independence and dependence, random variables, probability mass, density and distribution functions, standard distributions including Binomial, Poisson, Geometric, Normal, Uniform, Exponential, and Pareto, expectation and variance, variable transformations, Jensen's inequality, multivariate random variables, joint and marginal distributions, covariance and correlation, Chebychev's inequality, Law of Large Numbers, Central Limit Theorem, sampling theory, statistical models, mean, sample variance, histogram, empirical distribution function, boxplot, theory of estimators including bias, efficiency, mean squared error, linear regression (univariate and multivariate, categorical variables, goodness of fit), confidence intervals for mean and proportion, t-distribution, testing theory (Type I/II error, p-value, significance level, critical region, multiple testing, p-hacking), hypothesis tests on means, proportions, variances, and regression parameters, and interpretation of Python output in data analysis.

Main topics:
- Probability Theory
- Random Variables
- Statistical Inference
- Regression Analysis
- Sampling Theory
- Statistical Distributions
- Hypothesis Testing
- Estimation Theory

Keywords:
Conditional Probability, Bayes' Rule, Stochastic Independence, Probability Mass Function, Probability Density Function, Binomial Distribution, Poisson Distribution, Normal Distribution, Expectation, Variance, Jensen's Inequality, Covariance, Correlation, Central Limit Theorem, Linear Regression, t-distribution, Python

Course title: Software Quality and Testing
Course code: CSE1110
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 2
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Software Engineering

Course description:
This course explores essential software testing techniques and software design methods for building high-quality, testable software systems. Topics include testing principles, test levels, the testing pyramid, automation with JUnit, specification-based and structural testing, coverage criteria, boundary analysis, design by contract, property-based testing, use of test doubles and mock objects, design for testability, test-driven development (TDD), larger-scale tests such as SQL and web testing, test code quality, and identification of test smells. The course also addresses the use of tools like JUnit, Mockito, and JaCoCo, and architectural patterns such as dependency injection and ports and adapters.

Main topics:
- Software Testing
- Test Automation
- Software Design
- Test-Driven Development
- Structural Testing
- Specification-Based Testing
- Design by Contract
- Test Code Quality

Keywords:
JUnit, Mockito, JaCoCo, test doubles, mock objects, test coverage, boundary analysis, test pyramid, property-based testing, dependency injection, ports and adapters, test-driven development, test smells, integration testing, SQL testing

Course title: Computer Networks
Course code: CSE1405
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 2
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: English
Broad category: Networking

Course description:
Computer networks are structured in layers, each with specific functions and algorithms. The course covers the principles of layered architectures, the functions and purposes of various network layers, and the core algorithms used at each layer. It also addresses network security issues, cryptographic concepts for defense mechanisms, and introduces ethical hacking and responsible disclosure.

Main topics:
- Layered Network Architectures
- Network Protocols
- Network Security
- Cryptography
- Ethical Hacking

Keywords:
OSI Model, TCP/IP, Routing Algorithms, Switching, Encryption, Authentication, Firewalls, Intrusion Detection, Responsible Disclosure, Network Layers, Security Mitigation, Packet Switching, Network Attacks

Course title: Machine Learning
Course code: CSE2510
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 3
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course provides a broad introduction to machine learning and statistical pattern recognition. It covers supervised learning (generative and discriminative models, parametric and non-parametric methods, neural networks, support vector machines), unsupervised learning (clustering, dimensionality reduction, kernel methods), learning theory (including the bias–variance trade-off), and reinforcement learning. Recent applications of machine learning are discussed, such as robotic control, data mining, autonomous navigation, bioinformatics, speech recognition, and text and web data processing. After completing the course, students will be able to explain core machine learning concepts (including supervised and unsupervised learning, classification, regression, and clustering), apply standard algorithms to practical datasets, evaluate model performance using appropriate metrics, recognize limitations and ethical implications of machine learning systems, and implement machine learning algorithms using popular Python libraries.

Main topics:
- Supervised learning
- Unsupervised learning
- Classification
- Regression
- Clustering
- Neural networks
- Support vector machines
- Dimensionality reduction
- Kernel methods
- Learning theory
- Bias–variance trade-off
- Reinforcement learning
- Model evaluation metrics
- Ethics in machine learning
- Python for machine learning
- Applications in robotics
- Data mining
- Autonomous navigation
- Bioinformatics
- Speech recognition
- Text and web data processing

Keywords:
machine learning, statistical pattern recognition, supervised learning, unsupervised learning, classification, regression, clustering, generative models, discriminative models, parametric methods, non-parametric methods, neural networks, support vector machines, SVM, dimensionality reduction, kernel methods, learning theory, bias variance tradeoff, reinforcement learning, model evaluation, performance metrics, ethical implications, responsible AI, Python, machine learning libraries, robotic control, data mining, autonomous navigation, bioinformatics, speech recognition, text mining, web data processing

Course title: Operating Systems
Course code: CSE2140
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 3
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course introduces the fundamental concepts and mechanisms of modern operating systems, the core software layer between hardware and applications. Topics include process and thread management, CPU scheduling, synchronization and deadlocks, memory management (including virtual memory, paging, and segmentation), and storage management (file systems, mass-storage structures, and I/O systems), as well as protection and security. Students learn how operating systems manage resources, provide safe and efficient execution environments, and expose services to applications. Upon completion, students can describe the main responsibilities of an operating system, explain how processes and threads are created, scheduled, and synchronized, solve basic concurrency problems using synchronization primitives such as mutexes and semaphores, explain key memory management and virtual memory mechanisms, describe how file systems are structured and managed on disk, and write C programs that interact with the operating system through system calls.

Main topics:
- Operating system responsibilities and architecture
- Processes and threads
- CPU scheduling
- Process synchronization
- Deadlocks
- Main memory management
- Virtual memory, paging, and segmentation
- File systems and directory structures
- Mass-storage and I/O systems
- Protection and security
- System calls and OS interfaces
- C programming with system calls

Keywords:
operating systems, OS architecture, process management, threads, CPU scheduling, context switching, synchronization, mutexes, semaphores, critical section, deadlock, memory management, virtual memory, paging, segmentation, file systems, disk management, mass-storage structure, I/O systems, protection, security, system calls, C programming, resource management, concurrency

Course title: Artificial Intelligence
Course code: CSE2520
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 3
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the field of computer science focused on creating intelligent agents—systems that can reason, learn, and act autonomously. This course introduces the foundational principles of AI, including search algorithms (uninformed, informed, heuristic search, and adversarial search in games), logical agents (propositional logic, first-order logic, and inference), planning under certainty and uncertainty, and probabilistic reasoning (Bayesian networks and Hidden Markov Models). Upon completion, students will be able to formulate real-world problems as state-space search problems, apply various search algorithms to find optimal or near-optimal solutions, design intelligent agents that reason logically about their environment, represent uncertain knowledge using probabilistic models, and implement basic AI algorithms to solve practical problems.

Main topics:
- Artificial intelligence fundamentals
- Intelligent agents
- State-space search
- Uninformed search algorithms
- Informed and heuristic search
- Adversarial search and game playing
- Propositional logic
- First-order logic
- Logical inference
- Planning under certainty
- Planning under uncertainty
- Probabilistic reasoning
- Bayesian networks
- Hidden Markov Models
- Design and implementation of AI algorithms

Keywords:
artificial intelligence, intelligent agents, search algorithms, uninformed search, informed search, heuristic search, adversarial search, game playing, propositional logic, first-order logic, logical inference, planning, uncertainty, probabilistic reasoning, Bayesian networks, Hidden Markov Models, state-space search, AI algorithms

metadata.title: Artificial Intelligence
topics: Design and implementation of AI algorithms

content:
Course title: Concepts of Programming Languages
Course code: CSE2120
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 3
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
Programming languages are the primary tool of the software engineer. This course explores the fundamental concepts underlying the design and implementation of programming languages. Covered topics include formal syntax and semantics, lexical and syntax analysis (parsing), naming, bindings, and scopes, data types and type systems, control structures, subprograms and parameter passing mechanisms, object-oriented programming concepts, and functional programming concepts. After completing this course, students will be able to describe the different phases of compilation and interpretation, specify the syntax of a programming language using regular expressions and context-free grammars, explain the concepts of scope, lifetime, and binding, compare and contrast imperative, object-oriented, and functional programming paradigms, and write basic programs in a functional language such as Haskell.

Main topics:
- Programming language concepts
- Formal syntax and semantics
- Lexical analysis
- Parsing and syntax analysis
- Regular expressions
- Context-free grammars
- Scopes, naming, and bindings
- Lifetime and storage allocation
- Data types and type systems
- Control structures
- Subprograms and procedures
- Parameter passing mechanisms
- Imperative programming paradigm
- Object-oriented programming paradigm
- Functional programming paradigm
- Introduction to Haskell
- Compilation phases
- Interpretation

Keywords:
programming languages, language design, language implementation, formal syntax, formal semantics, lexical analysis, scanner, parser, syntax analysis, regular expressions, context-free grammar, CFG, naming, bindings, scope, lifetime, data types, type systems, static typing, dynamic typing, control flow, control structures, subprograms, procedures, functions, parameter passing, call by value, call by reference, object-oriented programming, OOP, classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, functional programming, higher-order functions, immutability, Haskell, compiler phases, interpretation, semantics of programming languages

Course title: Software Engineering Methods
Course code: CSE2115
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 4
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Software Engineering

Course description:
Developing large-scale software systems requires more than programming skills; it requires a disciplined engineering approach. This course introduces core software engineering methods, principles, and practices for building and maintaining complex software. Covered topics include software life cycle models (waterfall, iterative, agile), requirements engineering, software architecture and design patterns, software quality assurance and testing, project management, and ethical considerations in software engineering. After completing the course, students will be able to explain the advantages and disadvantages of different software development processes; elicit, analyze, and document software requirements; apply design patterns to solve common architectural problems; plan and track a software project using agile methodologies such as Scrum; and understand the importance of software quality and apply various testing strategies.

Main topics:
- Software engineering fundamentals
- Software development life cycle models
- Waterfall, iterative, and agile processes
- Requirements engineering
- Software architecture
- Design patterns
- Software quality assurance
- Software testing strategies
- Agile project management
- Scrum methodology
- Project planning and tracking
- Ethics in software engineering
- Large-scale software systems

Keywords:
software engineering, software development process, software life cycle, waterfall model, iterative development, agile development, Scrum, requirements engineering, requirements analysis, requirements documentation, software architecture, design patterns, architectural patterns, software quality, quality assurance, software testing, testing strategies, project management, project planning, project tracking, ethical issues, large-scale systems, CSE2115, TUDelft

Course title: Information Security
Course code: CSE2530
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 4
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
As reliance on computer systems grows, so does the need to secure them. This course offers a comprehensive introduction to information security. It covers basic cryptography (symmetric and asymmetric encryption, hash functions, digital signatures), network security (firewalls, intrusion detection systems, TLS, IPsec), web security (SQL injection, cross-site scripting, cross-site request forgery), system security (access control, malware, buffer overflows), and security management (risk assessment, security policies). Upon completion, students will be able to identify common security vulnerabilities in software and networked systems, explain the principles of applied cryptography, design and implement basic security mechanisms, analyze the security posture of a system, and understand the ethical and legal implications of information security.

Main topics:
- Information security fundamentals
- Applied cryptography
- Symmetric and asymmetric encryption
- Hash functions and digital signatures
- Network security
- Firewalls and intrusion detection systems
- TLS and IPsec
- Web application security
- SQL injection
- Cross-site scripting (XSS)
- Cross-site request forgery (CSRF)
- System and software security
- Access control mechanisms
- Malware and buffer overflows
- Security management and governance
- Risk assessment and security policies
- Ethical and legal aspects of security
- Vulnerability analysis and threat modeling

Keywords:
information security, cybersecurity, cryptography, symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, public key cryptography, hash function, digital signature, network security, firewall, intrusion detection system, IDS, TLS, IPsec, web security, SQL injection, XSS, cross-site scripting, CSRF, cross-site request forgery, system security, access control, malware, buffer overflow, security management, risk assessment, security policy, vulnerability analysis, ethical issues, legal issues, secure system design

metadata.title: Information Security
topics: Ethical and legal aspects of security

content:
Course title: Automata, Computability and Complexity
Course code: CSE2225
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 4
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course studies the theoretical foundations of computer science by asking what can and cannot be computed by a machine, and how much time and memory are required for computation. Students learn about finite automata and regular languages, pushdown automata and context-free languages, Turing machines and the Church–Turing thesis, decidability and the halting problem, and computational complexity classes such as P, NP, and NP-completeness, including reductions. After completing the course, students will be able to design finite automata and regular expressions for regular languages, construct pushdown automata and context-free grammars, explain the concept of Turing-computability, prove that certain problems are undecidable, and apply reductions to prove NP-completeness of computational problems.

Main topics:
- Finite automata
- Regular languages
- Regular expressions
- Pushdown automata
- Context-free languages
- Context-free grammars
- Turing machines
- Church–Turing thesis
- Decidability
- Halting problem
- Computational complexity
- Complexity classes P and NP
- NP-completeness
- Reductions
- Undecidability proofs

Keywords:
automata theory, finite automata, DFA, NFA, regular languages, regular expressions, pushdown automata, PDA, context-free languages, context-free grammars, CFG, Turing machines, Church–Turing thesis, decidability, undecidable problems, halting problem, computational complexity, time complexity, space complexity, complexity classes, P, NP, NP-complete, NP-completeness, reductions, polynomial-time reduction, theoretical computer science, computability theory

Course title: Computer Graphics
Course code: CSE2215
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 4
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course introduces the principles of computer graphics, focusing on the generation of 2D and 3D images. Students study the graphics pipeline, geometric transformations (translation, rotation, scaling), viewing and projections, and object representations such as meshes, curves, and surfaces. The course covers color models, illumination and shading (including the Phong model), rasterization, ray tracing, and texture mapping. Upon completion, students will be able to describe the mathematical foundations of computer graphics, apply 3D transformations to manipulate objects in a virtual scene, explain the differences between rasterization and ray tracing, implement basic shading models to render realistic surfaces, and use a standard graphics API (e.g., OpenGL or WebGL) to create interactive 3D applications.

Main topics:
- Computer graphics fundamentals
- 2D and 3D image generation
- Graphics pipeline
- Geometric transformations
- Viewing and projection
- Object representation
- Meshes, curves, and surfaces
- Color models
- Illumination and shading
- Phong shading model
- Rasterization
- Ray tracing
- Texture mapping
- Mathematical foundations of graphics
- Interactive 3D applications
- Graphics APIs (OpenGL, WebGL)

Keywords:
computer graphics, 2D graphics, 3D graphics, graphics pipeline, geometric transformations, translation, rotation, scaling, viewing, projections, object representation, meshes, curves, surfaces, color models, illumination, shading, Phong model, rasterization, ray tracing, texture mapping, virtual scene, shading models, OpenGL, WebGL, interactive 3D applications

Course title: Human-Computer Interaction
Course code: CSE2535
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 5
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course introduces the principles and practices of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), focusing on how to design software that people can use effectively and efficiently. Students learn the full user interface design process, including understanding human perception and cognition, applying user-centered design methodologies, and performing task analysis to understand user goals and workflows. The course covers different interaction styles, core UI design principles and heuristics, and a range of prototyping techniques such as paper prototypes, wireframes, and interactive prototypes. Students also study and apply usability evaluation methods, including heuristic evaluation and user testing. By the end of the course, students are able to analyze user needs and contexts of use, design intuitive and accessible user interfaces, create prototypes of varying fidelity, conduct usability evaluations to identify interface problems, and iteratively improve designs based on user feedback.

Main topics:
- Human-Computer Interaction fundamentals
- Human perception and cognition in interface design
- User-centered design methodologies
- Task analysis and modeling
- Interaction styles and interaction paradigms
- User interface design principles and heuristics
- Prototyping techniques: paper, wireframes, interactive
- Usability evaluation methods
- Heuristic evaluation
- User testing and user studies
- Iterative design and refinement
- Accessibility and inclusive design

Keywords:
Human-Computer Interaction, HCI, user interface design, user-centered design, human perception, human cognition, task analysis, interaction styles, UI heuristics, design principles, prototyping, paper prototyping, wireframes, interactive prototypes, usability evaluation, heuristic evaluation, user testing, usability testing, iterative design, accessibility, user experience, UX

metadata.title: Human-Computer Interaction
topics: Human perception and cognition in interface design
User interface design principles and heuristics

content:
Course title: Big Data Processing
Course code: CSE2540
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 5
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
In the modern era, the volume of data generated often exceeds the processing capabilities of a single machine. This course introduces frameworks and paradigms for distributed data processing. Topics include the challenges of big data, the MapReduce programming model, distributed file systems (HDFS), the Apache Spark framework (RDDs, DataFrames, Spark SQL), stream processing, and NoSQL databases for large-scale data storage. After completing this course, students can explain the principles of distributed data processing, write MapReduce programs to process large datasets, develop data processing pipelines using Apache Spark, analyze streaming data in real time, and select appropriate data storage and processing technologies for different big data scenarios.

Main topics:
- Big data challenges
- Distributed data processing
- MapReduce programming model
- Distributed file systems
- HDFS
- Apache Spark
- RDDs
- DataFrames
- Spark SQL
- Stream processing
- Real-time data analysis
- NoSQL databases
- Large-scale data storage
- Data processing pipelines
- Scalable data architectures

Keywords:
big data, distributed computing, MapReduce, HDFS, Apache Spark, Spark RDD, Spark DataFrame, Spark SQL, stream processing, real-time analytics, NoSQL, large-scale data processing, data pipelines, cluster computing, data-intensive applications

Course title: Data Mining
Course code: CSE2525
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 4
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
In the information age, extracting meaningful patterns from vast amounts of data is a critical skill. This course introduces the fundamental concepts and algorithms of data mining and knowledge discovery. Covered topics include data preprocessing and cleaning, frequent pattern mining and association rules (Apriori, FP-growth), advanced clustering techniques (hierarchical and density-based), anomaly and outlier detection, and recommendation systems. After completing this course, students will be able to explain the knowledge discovery process, preprocess raw data to make it suitable for mining, apply association rule mining to find frequent patterns in transactional databases, design and evaluate recommendation systems, and use data mining software and libraries to analyze real-world datasets.

Main topics:
- Data mining fundamentals
- Knowledge discovery process
- Data preprocessing and cleaning
- Frequent pattern mining
- Association rule mining
- Apriori algorithm
- FP-growth algorithm
- Clustering techniques
- Hierarchical clustering
- Density-based clustering
- Anomaly detection
- Outlier detection
- Recommendation systems
- Practical use of data mining software and libraries
- Analysis of real-world datasets

Keywords:
data mining, knowledge discovery, data preprocessing, data cleaning, frequent pattern mining, association rules, Apriori, FP-growth, clustering, hierarchical clustering, density-based clustering, DBSCAN, anomaly detection, outlier detection, recommendation systems, recommender systems, transactional databases, pattern discovery, data analysis, machine learning

metadata.title: Data Mining
topics: Practical use of data mining software and libraries

Course title: Compiler Construction
Course code: CSE2230
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 5
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course explains how high-level programming languages are translated into machine code, covering both theoretical foundations and practical aspects of compiler design. Students study lexical analysis and regular expressions, syntax analysis with top-down and bottom-up parsing techniques, semantic analysis and type checking, intermediate representation (IR) generation, code optimization, and target code generation. By the end of the course, students are able to describe the architecture of a modern compiler, use parser generators to construct parsers for context-free grammars, implement semantic rules and symbol tables, apply basic code optimization techniques such as constant folding and dead code elimination, and build a functional compiler for a simple programming language.

Main topics:
- Compiler architecture
- Lexical analysis
- Scanning and regular expressions
- Syntax analysis
- Top-down parsing
- Bottom-up parsing
- Context-free grammars
- Semantic analysis
- Type checking
- Symbol tables
- Intermediate representation (IR)
- Code optimization
- Constant folding
- Dead code elimination
- Target code generation
- Parser generators
- Compiler implementation for simple languages

Keywords:
compiler construction, compiler design, lexical analysis, scanner, regular expressions, syntax analysis, parsing, top-down parser, bottom-up parser, LL parsing, LR parsing, context-free grammar, semantic analysis, type checking, symbol table, intermediate representation, IR generation, code optimization, constant folding, dead code elimination, target code generation, parser generator, compiler implementation, programming languages, machine code

Course title: Embedded Systems
Course code: CSE2155
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 4
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Engineering

Course description:
Embedded systems are ubiquitous, appearing in devices ranging from household appliances to automotive control systems. This course explores the hardware and software design of such systems. Students study microcontroller architectures and instruction sets, memory mapping, and peripheral interfacing (including GPIO, ADCs, and timers). The course covers interrupt handling, real-time constraints, and serial communication protocols such as I2C, SPI, and UART, as well as the principles and use of Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS). Upon completion, students will be able to program microcontrollers in C and assembly, interface hardware components with a microcontroller, design software that meets strict real-time deadlines, explain the role of an RTOS and its scheduling mechanisms, and develop a complete embedded system prototype.

Main topics:
- Embedded systems fundamentals
- Microcontroller architectures
- Instruction sets
- Memory mapping
- Peripheral interfacing
- GPIO
- ADCs and timers
- Interrupt handling
- Real-time constraints
- Serial communication protocols
- I2C
- SPI
- UART
- Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)
- RTOS scheduling mechanisms
- C programming for microcontrollers
- Assembly programming for microcontrollers
- Hardware-software integration
- Embedded system prototyping

Keywords:
embedded systems, microcontroller, instruction set, memory mapping, peripheral interfacing, GPIO, ADC, timers, interrupts, real-time systems, real-time constraints, serial communication, I2C, SPI, UART, RTOS, real-time operating system, scheduling, C programming, assembly language, hardware interface, embedded prototype, automotive control, embedded software design, embedded hardware design

Course title: Computer Vision
Course code: CSE2545
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 5
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
Computer Vision enables machines to interpret and understand the visual world. This advanced course bridges traditional image processing and modern deep learning approaches. Topics include image filtering and morphological operations, feature detection and description (SIFT, SURF, ORB), camera calibration and epipolar geometry, object detection and image segmentation, and state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for visual tasks. After completing this course, students can implement basic image processing algorithms, extract robust features from images for matching and tracking, explain the geometry of multiple views and stereo vision, train and fine-tune CNNs for image classification and object detection, and apply computer vision techniques to solve practical problems.

Main topics:
- Image processing
- Image filtering
- Morphological operations
- Feature detection and description
- SIFT
- SURF
- ORB
- Camera calibration
- Epipolar geometry
- Multiple view geometry
- Stereo vision
- Object detection
- Image segmentation
- Convolutional Neural Networks
- Deep learning for vision
- Image classification
- Visual tracking
- Practical computer vision applications

Keywords:
computer vision, image processing, image filtering, morphological operations, feature detection, feature description, SIFT, SURF, ORB, camera calibration, epipolar geometry, multiple views, stereo vision, object detection, image segmentation, CNN, convolutional neural networks, deep learning, image classification, visual tracking, TUDelft, CSE2545

Course title: Natural Language Processing
Course code: CSE2550
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 6
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course introduces Natural Language Processing (NLP) at the intersection of computer science, artificial intelligence, and linguistics. Students learn how computers process and analyze large-scale natural language data, from basic text preprocessing to modern deep learning approaches. The course covers text normalization and tokenization, n-gram language models, word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe), sequence modeling with Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and LSTMs, attention mechanisms, and Transformer architectures. Applications such as machine translation and sentiment analysis are discussed. After completing the course, students will be able to preprocess raw text for machine learning, train and evaluate language models, explain the architecture of Transformer-based networks (e.g., BERT, GPT), and implement NLP pipelines for classification and text generation tasks.

Main topics:
- Natural language processing fundamentals
- Text preprocessing and normalization
- Tokenization
- N-gram language models
- Word embeddings
- Word2Vec
- GloVe
- Recurrent Neural Networks
- LSTMs
- Sequence modeling
- Attention mechanisms
- Transformer architectures
- BERT
- GPT
- Machine translation
- Sentiment analysis
- Text classification
- Text generation
- NLP pipelines
- Language model training and evaluation

Keywords:
NLP, natural language processing, text preprocessing, text normalization, tokenization, n-gram models, language models, word embeddings, Word2Vec, GloVe, RNN, LSTM, sequence modeling, attention, Transformers, BERT, GPT, machine translation, sentiment analysis, text classification, text generation, deep learning for NLP

Course title: Software Architecture
Course code: CSE2125
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 5
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This course introduces the principles and practices of software architecture as the foundation of large-scale software systems. It covers high-level system structures, architectural styles and patterns (such as Layered, Client-Server, Microservices, and Event-Driven), and architectural tactics for achieving quality attributes including performance, security, and availability. Students learn to document software architectures using UML and architectural views (e.g., the 4+1 view model), and to evaluate and compare architectural alternatives. After completing the course, students will be able to identify the architectural style of existing systems, design architectures that satisfy functional and non-functional requirements, document architectural decisions clearly and professionally, and assess the trade-offs of different architectural choices.

Main topics:
- Software architecture fundamentals
- Architectural styles and patterns
- Layered architecture
- Client-server architecture
- Microservices architecture
- Event-driven architecture
- Quality attributes and architectural tactics
- Performance, security, and availability
- UML for architecture documentation
- Architectural views and 4+1 view model
- Architecture evaluation and trade-off analysis
- Designing architectures for requirements
- Documenting architectural decisions

Keywords:
software architecture, architectural styles, architectural patterns, layered architecture, client-server, microservices, event-driven, quality attributes, performance, security, availability, architectural tactics, UML, architectural views, 4+1 view model, architecture documentation, architecture evaluation, trade-off analysis, non-functional requirements, architectural decisions

Course title: Algorithm Design
Course code: CSE2310
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 5
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
This advanced course builds upon basic data structures and algorithms, focusing on sophisticated techniques for designing efficient algorithms. Topics include advanced divide-and-conquer strategies, dynamic programming (memoization and tabulation), greedy algorithms and matroid theory, network flow algorithms (such as Ford-Fulkerson and maximum bipartite matching), string matching algorithms, and randomized algorithms. After completing this course, students will be able to identify problems that can be solved using dynamic programming or greedy approaches, formulate real-world problems as network flow or graph problems, rigorously prove the correctness and analyze the complexity of complex algorithms, and implement advanced algorithmic techniques to solve computationally hard problems efficiently.

Main topics:
- Advanced divide-and-conquer
- Dynamic programming
- Greedy algorithms
- Matroid theory
- Network flow algorithms
- Ford-Fulkerson algorithm
- Maximum bipartite matching
- String matching algorithms
- Randomized algorithms
- Algorithm correctness proofs
- Complexity analysis
- Graph algorithms

Keywords:
algorithm design, divide and conquer, dynamic programming, memoization, tabulation, greedy algorithms, matroids, matroid theory, network flow, max flow, Ford-Fulkerson, bipartite matching, maximum bipartite matching, string matching, pattern matching, randomized algorithms, algorithm correctness, time complexity, space complexity, graph problems, computationally hard problems

Course title: Quantum Computing
Course code: CSE2240
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 6
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
Quantum computing represents a paradigm shift in computation, promising exponential speedups for certain classes of problems. This introductory course covers the foundations of quantum information and quantum algorithms. Students learn about qubits, superposition, and entanglement; quantum gates and quantum circuits; and the mathematical formalism of quantum mechanics, including linear algebra and Hilbert spaces. The course introduces key quantum algorithms such as the Deutsch–Jozsa algorithm, Grover's search algorithm, and Shor's factoring algorithm, as well as the basics of quantum error correction. After completing this course, students will be able to perform calculations involving quantum states and gates, design simple quantum circuits, explain how quantum algorithms achieve speedups over classical counterparts, and understand the physical and theoretical limitations of current quantum computers.

Main topics:
- Quantum information
- Qubits and quantum states
- Superposition and entanglement
- Quantum gates
- Quantum circuits
- Linear algebra for quantum computing
- Hilbert spaces
- Deutsch–Jozsa algorithm
- Grover's search algorithm
- Shor's factoring algorithm
- Quantum algorithms
- Quantum error correction
- Limits of current quantum computers

Keywords:
quantum computing, quantum information, qubits, superposition, entanglement, quantum gates, quantum circuits, linear algebra, Hilbert space, Deutsch–Jozsa, Grover's search, Shor's algorithm, quantum algorithms, quantum error correction, computational speedup, quantum complexity, noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices

Course title: Robotics
Course code: CSE2560
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 6
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Robotics and Control

Course description:
This course introduces the mechanics, control, and programming of robotic systems, with emphasis on mobile robots and robotic manipulators. Students learn rigid body transformations and kinematics (forward and inverse), robot dynamics and control, and sensor processing using lidar, vision, and odometry. The course covers probabilistic robotics methods such as Kalman filters and particle filters, as well as Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM), path planning, and obstacle avoidance. Students also gain practical experience with the Robot Operating System (ROS) for simulating and controlling robotic systems. Upon completion, students can derive kinematic models for robotic arms and mobile bases, implement state estimation algorithms to track a robot’s pose, design motion planners for navigation in complex environments, and use ROS in a robotics software pipeline.

Main topics:
- Rigid body transformations
- Forward and inverse kinematics
- Robot dynamics
- Robot control
- Mobile robotics
- Robotic manipulators
- Sensor processing
- Lidar-based perception
- Computer vision for robotics
- Odometry
- Probabilistic robotics
- Kalman filtering
- Particle filtering
- Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)
- Path planning
- Obstacle avoidance
- Robot Operating System (ROS)
- State estimation
- Motion planning
- Robot simulation

Keywords:
robotics, mobile robots, robotic manipulators, rigid body transformations, kinematics, forward kinematics, inverse kinematics, robot dynamics, robot control, sensors, lidar, vision, odometry, probabilistic robotics, Kalman filter, particle filter, SLAM, Simultaneous Localization and Mapping, path planning, motion planning, obstacle avoidance, state estimation, ROS, Robot Operating System, robot simulation, navigation, pose estimation

Course title: Blockchain Technologies
Course code: CSE2570
University: TUDelft
Country: NL
Semester: 6
ECTS: 5
Language of source content: en
Broad category: Computer Science

Course description:
Blockchain technology and distributed ledgers have applications far beyond cryptocurrencies. This course covers the technical foundations of decentralized systems, including cryptographic hash functions and digital signatures, peer-to-peer networks, and consensus algorithms such as Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, and Byzantine Fault Tolerance. Students study the architecture of major blockchain platforms like Bitcoin and Ethereum, and learn smart contract development in Solidity, the design of decentralized applications (DApps), and privacy and scaling solutions such as zero-knowledge proofs and layer-2 networks. After completing the course, students will be able to explain the mechanics of blockchain consensus and state replication, identify scenarios where blockchain offers advantages over centralized databases, write, test, and deploy secure smart contracts, and build a simple DApp that interacts with a blockchain network.

Main topics:
- Blockchain fundamentals
- Distributed ledgers
- Cryptographic hash functions
- Digital signatures
- Peer-to-peer networks
- Consensus algorithms
- Proof of Work
- Proof of Stake
- Byzantine Fault Tolerance
- Bitcoin architecture
- Ethereum architecture
- Smart contracts
- Solidity programming
- Decentralized applications (DApps)
- Zero-knowledge proofs
- Layer-2 scaling solutions
- Blockchain privacy
- State replication
- Decentralized vs centralized databases
- Secure smart contract development

Keywords:
blockchain, distributed ledger, cryptography, hash function, digital signature, peer-to-peer network, consensus, Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, Byzantine Fault Tolerance, Bitcoin, Ethereum, smart contract, Solidity, DApp, decentralized application, zero-knowledge proof, layer-2, scaling, privacy, state replication, decentralized system, centralized database, secure coding
